segments.title: Military necessity and the waste of life
segments.summary: The opening distinguishes unavoidable wartime sacrifice from an operation too small and poorly conceived to achieve its stated aims.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke frames Tan No. 2 as an ethical failure rooted in operational failure, not simply as another dangerous mission. He allows that states may have to fight when aggression threatens others, but argues that commanders still owe personnel a viable military purpose. In this case, the assigned force was too small and the planning too weak to offer a plausible route to the stated objective. The passage also separates criticism of this particular raid from the broader cultural and historical question of why Japan adopted kamikaze operations.

evidence.note: The introduction contrasts necessary resistance in war with an operation that lacked the forces and viability to achieve its aims.

segments.title: Operation Y-Go and the limits of delaying victory
segments.summary: Operation Y-Go is used to show that even a successful surprise attack on American carriers could only delay, not reverse, the Pacific war.
segments.body: The discussion compares a hoped-for carrier raid with Pearl Harbor while stressing the changed industrial balance of 1944. American carrier losses could be replaced in months, whereas Japan could not restore equivalent strength. British carrier construction also meant that Allied naval aviation was not confined to the American replacement program. The contrast frames Tan No. 2 as an attempt to gain time without a credible path to changing the war's outcome.

evidence.note: The lecture explains Y-Go, replacement capacity, and why a carrier strike could only delay the war.

segments.title: Kantai Kessen and the danger of planning for a foolish enemy
segments.summary: The failure of Kantai Kessen becomes a warning against strategies that work only if an opponent keeps making mistakes.
segments.body: The lecture treats the Philippine Sea as the last opportunity for the decisive-battle concept and a demonstration of its weakness. A viable plan must assume a capable, committed opponent rather than one who keeps advancing into an arranged trap. Strategy may be adapted to one's own circumstances, but it must still account realistically for the enemy's intelligence, equipment, and will. That principle matters because Tan No. 2 demanded American complacency while also demanding perfect Japanese execution.

evidence.note: The lecture links Kantai Kessen to assumptions about enemy behaviour and strategic preparation.

segments.title: Operation Tan's small-strike problem
segments.summary: An earlier plan for just 54 aircraft reveals how little margin Japan had for a surprise raid on a major anchorage.
segments.body: The original Operation Tan concept called for reconnaissance from Truk followed by 27 torpedo bombers and 27 dive bombers. Its prospects depended on unusual surprise and still offered no certainty of major results. The discussion explains why shallow-water torpedo attack required more than simply altering the torpedo. British practice at Taranto combined tail fins with a tension wire that made the weapon enter the water in a flatter attitude, a method whose physical traces were easy to overlook after an air raid.

evidence.note: The lecture describes the 54-aircraft concept and the technical problem of torpedo attacks in harbour.

segments.title: C6N reconnaissance undone by serviceability
segments.summary: The C6N Saiun promised long-range reconnaissance, but the move to Truk left only one aircraft reliably available.
segments.body: The C6N was designed to solve the limitations of cruiser floatplane reconnaissance, but it required careful maintenance and specialized support. Of seven aircraft sent toward Truk, two turned back with engine trouble, one ditched en route, and another was wrecked on landing. The base then lacked the specialized servicing, fuel, and spares needed to keep the survivors available. The resulting reconnaissance plan depended on one serviceable aircraft and a submarine-delivered supply effort.

evidence.note: The lecture describes the C6N's role, the delivery losses, and the lack of maintenance support at Truk.

segments.title: Ulithi and the wrong scale for a penny-packet raid
segments.summary: Ulithi's immense anchorage exposes the mismatch between the American fleet base and Japan's small proposed raid.
segments.body: Ulithi had become a major forward base with capacity for an enormous fleet and facilities such as floating dry docks. Supply ships, tugs, escorts, cruisers, destroyers, battleships, and several types of carrier filled the anchorage. The lecture argues that counting only the carriers hid the depth of the force being targeted. A small force sent piecemeal against such a concentration would be expended without producing strategically meaningful damage, much as repeated small attacks fail once a defended opponent has concentrated strength.

evidence.note: The lecture describes Ulithi's facilities and fleet concentration, then criticizes penny-packet attacks.

segments.title: Optimism bias and serviceability planning
segments.summary: The C6N deployment becomes a case study in why planners must scale forces around observed serviceability rather than perfect performance.
segments.body: The plan assumed that every Japanese component would work while the American defenders remained unprepared. Actual C6N experience gave a stark warning: only one of seven dispatched reconnaissance aircraft was serviceable at Truk after losses, defects, and inadequate support. Dr. Clarke argues that this observed rate should have driven force sizing and logistics calculations rather than being treated as an exception. The intended targets were the carriers enabling American amphibious operations, so treating readiness data as optional undermined the raid's strategic premise. The passage turns Tan No. 2 into a broader lesson about optimism bias, readiness data, and the danger of planning around ideal conditions.

evidence.note: The lecture contrasts the plan's perfect-performance assumptions with the roughly one-in-seven C6N serviceability actually observed.

segments.title: Tan No. 2's strike plan had no fuel margin
segments.summary: The planned 24 Ginga kamikaze strike depended on a long weather-avoidance route that made fuel itself part of the weapon's shortfall.
segments.body: The strike force included P1Y Ginga bombers, H8K flying boats for weather and guidance, and submarines assigned to support roles. Avoiding equatorial storms lengthened the route and consumed fuel before the aircraft reached Ulithi. The lecture stresses that a kamikaze aircraft's remaining fuel contributed to its destructive effect, so this was not merely a range problem. Because an unsuspecting anchorage would be no more alert against 240 or 480 aircraft than against 24, Dr. Clarke argues that Japan either needed to concentrate enough striking power to matter or abandon the raid. Against the fleet at Ulithi, 24 aircraft were neither a viable mass attack nor a force worth sacrificing in isolation.

evidence.note: The lecture outlines the P1Y and H8K force, the route, fuel burden, and inadequate strike size.

segments.title: Submarine support and rigid planning
segments.summary: The submarine plan added logistics, navigation, and rescue tasks while mechanical failures and rigid orders eroded its usefulness.
segments.body: Three submarines were assigned to rescue, supply, and navigation support, making the air operation dependent on another fragile chain. I-366 had to return for repairs after receiving extra radar equipment and a formal send-off. I-58 was recalled from a Kaiten mission only about 90 minutes from its release point, forcing two Kaiten to be abandoned without any chance to attack. The episode illustrates how adherence to a written plan displaced practical judgement about sequencing tasks and using the forces already at hand.

evidence.note: The lecture details the submarine assignments, I-366's defects, and I-58's recall.

segments.title: Reconnaissance of Ulithi and a fragile launch
segments.summary: A C6N reconnaissance flight identified carriers at Ulithi, but bad weather and engine failures began dismantling the strike force on departure.
segments.body: The 5 March reconnaissance found a Saratoga, four Essex-class carriers, three Independence-class carriers, seven escort carriers, and other unidentified ships. The C6N survived interception by three P-38 Lightnings, but the sighting confirmed the scale of the intended target. On D-Day, the H8K pathfinders were delayed by weather and the Ginga launch was already behind schedule. Within hours, five aircraft had turned back and three had crashed, reducing the force before it approached the target.

evidence.note: The lecture covers the C6N sighting, the D-Day launch, weather delays, and early attrition.

segments.title: Navigation error, attrition, and the hit on USS Randolph
segments.summary: Engine trouble and a navigation error left only two aircraft at Ulithi, where one struck USS Randolph with little fuel remaining.
segments.body: The strike shrank from 24 aircraft to eight before its navigational error near Yap, and only two eventually reached Ulithi after almost eleven hours in the air. One P1Y hit the Essex-class carrier USS Randolph, killing 26 and wounding 105, but its fuel was so depleted that the aircraft did not burst into flames. Japan paid for this temporary damage with roughly 60 to 70 personnel and at least 13 aircraft. Randolph returned for the Okinawa campaign, showing that the hit produced no durable operational result. The outcome is especially damning because the American anchorage had in fact been caught napping: the condition on which surprise depended was met, yet the understrength raid still failed. The other aircraft crashed into a baseball field without damaging a military target.

evidence.note: The lecture recounts the losses, the navigation error, the Randolph strike, the Japanese cost, and the failure despite achieving surprise.

segments.title: Heavier bombers and the payload alternative
segments.summary: A counterfactual comparison with the Doolittle Raid asks whether larger bombers could have delivered enough fuel and explosives to threaten Ulithi's carriers.
segments.body: The lecture asks whether merely sending more P1Y aircraft would have mattered when the route left them almost dry. A more dangerous alternative might have adapted larger medium bombers in the manner of the American Doolittle Raid. Greater payload and residual fuel could have produced much more destructive impacts on carrier decks. This remains a counterfactual design argument, but it clarifies that the plan's weakness lay in weapon effect as well as aircraft numbers.

evidence.note: The lecture considers a Doolittle-style use of larger bombers carrying more explosives and fuel as an alternative strike design.

segments.title: Big Blue Blanket and carrier damage resilience
segments.summary: Okinawa's Big Blue Blanket and carrier damage images show how air defence, fuel, repeated impacts, and construction shaped kamikaze consequences.
segments.body: Images from Okinawa provide the comparison that the lightly fueled Randolph strike could not. The Big Blue Blanket represented the development of air-defence doctrine intended to give carrier task groups relative, though not complete, safety. Bunker Hill suffered devastating damage from multiple kamikaze hits with much more fuel involved. Dr. Clarke then contrasts the Essex design with the greater structural resilience expected from a Midway-class or British armoured-hangar carrier. The point is not that any carrier was invulnerable, but that air defence, payload, number of hits, and protection all determined whether an attack produced lasting operational loss.

evidence.note: The lecture links the Big Blue Blanket's air-defence doctrine with Okinawa kamikaze damage and the protection of Essex, Midway, and British armoured-hangar carrier designs.

segments.title: Carrier resilience and the forlorn-hope test
segments.summary: The aftermath contrasts carrier resilience with a true forlorn hope: extreme risk is defensible only when it can unlock a concrete gain.
segments.body: Japanese reports initially imagined a second Pearl Harbor, but follow-up reconnaissance found the carriers still present and no visible fuel in the water. The lecture compares the raid with the British military tradition of the forlorn hope, whose volunteers accepted extreme danger to seize a wall and open the way for the main force. Such an assault had a concrete operational reward and could be sized into several parties rather than treated as sacrifice for its own sake. Dr. Clarke's final criticism is that Japan had more aircraft available yet committed too few to make the risk operationally meaningful. Tan No. 2 lacked both adequate resources and a credible payoff, making its loss of life strategically as well as morally indefensible.

evidence.note: The lecture covers the false expectation of a second Pearl Harbor and contrasts Tan No. 2 with a forlorn hope that could unlock a concrete military gain.
